course_description: The Honours program in the School of Historical and Philosophical Inquiry offers an opportunity to develop advanced research skills and make an original contribution to knowledge in the chosen discipline. It provides a significant competitive advantage for graduates. Honours is also an essential prerequisite for Higher Degree Research (MPhil or PhD). This course is the thesis component for students commencing Honours full-time in semester one in Classics and Ancient History (including Ancient History, Greek, and Latin), History, Philosophy, or Religious Studies. It provides skills and experience in research and thesis-writing.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:
restrictions: Required GPA of 5.5 in the relevant major/discipline

course_aims: This course aims to provide students with research expertise, analytical skills, and abilities in verbal and written communication.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand at a deeper level the complex nature of problems and methods in your discipline.
Reflect critically on arguments presented in contemporary and historical debates.
Demonstrate reasoned thought, creativity, and a high level of written expression.

assessment_summary.title: Honours Thesis
assessment_summary.due_date: 16/10/2026 2:00 pm; The thesis is due on Friday in Week 11 in the second semester of enrolment.

assessment_summary.title: Oral Defence
assessment_summary.due_date: The oral defence will take place after your thesis has been submitted and examined. Typically this will occur in Week 13 or the exam period of your submitting semester. The oral defence will take place in-person (face to face) and you will be advised of the time and location in advance.

assessment_details.title: Honours Thesis
assessment_details.due_date: 16/10/2026 2:00 pm The thesis is due on Friday in Week 11 in the second semester of enrolment.
assessment_details.task_description: The requirements for the Honours thesis vary by discipline. Your supervisor and Discipline Honours Advisor will provide more detailed information and guidance. Students in Ancient History write one thesis of 20,000 words. Students in Greek or Latin write one thesis of 16,000 words plus original translations. Students in History write one thesis of 16,000–20,000 words. Students in Philosophy write one thesis of 16,000–20,000 words. Students in Religious Studies write one thesis of 16,000–20,000 words. All word counts are +/- 10%, excluding front matter, references, and bibliography, but including discursive footnotes. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessment_details.title: Oral Defence
assessment_details.due_date: The oral defence will take place after your thesis has been submitted and examined. Typically this will occur in Week 13 or the exam period of your submitting semester. The oral defence will take place in-person (face to face) and you will be advised of the time and location in advance.
assessment_details.task_description: The oral defence will be marked on a pass/fail basis and be assessed against the following criteria: Demonstrated ability to articulate the rationale underpinning the choice of central research question/s, the identification of a key gap in knowledge that the thesis addresses, and the devising of the central hypothesis. Demonstrated ability to justify the thesis structure. Demonstrated ability to explain and justify the methodology/ies used. Demonstrated mastery of knowledge, analysis and findings presented in the thesis. Ability to satisfactorily respond to spontaneous and pertinent questions in the oral defence. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Oral defence will be delivered in-person
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If you are unable to attend your oral defence due to sudden illness and/or exceptional circumstances, you are required to notify the Honours Coordinator and your Supervisor as soon as possible and prior to the scheduled time of your defence (where possible).
A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time.
Please note, if you are approved an extension on your written thesis, your oral defence will also be delayed. The school will advise you as soon as possible of the revised date and time of the oral defence in the event of an approved extension of the written thesis.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 24 (23 Feb - 18 Oct)
learning_activities.topic: Thesis research and writing Students research and write their Honours theses, in consultation with their thesis advisor. Early in semester students should meet with their advisor to establish an agreed schedule for regular meetings and completion of agreed tasks. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
